message.content:
I'm building a knowledge graph visualization for a TypeScript static site generator (similar to Quartz by jackyzha0). I need to understand how to implement a force-directed knowledge graph WITHOUT any external npm dependencies (everything must be inline in the HTML output).

Please search the web and find:
1. How Quartz implements its graph view - specifically what library/approach it uses (D3? custom?)
2. The minimum D3.js code needed for a force-directed graph (just the force simulation part, not the full library) - approximately how many KB?
3. Alternative: can we implement a simple force-directed graph in pure vanilla JS + SVG without D3? What would that look like for ~100-500 nodes?
4. What is the typical data format for a knowledge graph (nodes + edges JSON)?
5. How does Quartz implement popover previews on hover - pure CSS or JS?

Also look at:
- src/report/render/links.ts at C:/Users/ai/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow - what link graph data is already built
- src/report/render/search.ts - how the inline JS search is currently embedded

Goal: design the simplest possible self-contained graph view that works from file:// and GitHub Pages with no CDN dependencies.

content.text: I'll help you research knowledge graph visualization for your static site generator. Let me search for information about Quartz's approach and then examine your existing codebase.

Links: [{"title":"The D3 Graph Gallery – Simple charts made with d3.js","url":"https://d3-graph-gallery.com/"},{"title":"D3","url":"https://d3js.org/"},{"title":"Visualization of knowledge graph (relational data) using D3 and React | Deus Ex Machina","url":"https://deus-ex-machina-ism.com/?p=21358&lang=en"},{"title":"Knowledge Schema Graph of D3 | Download Scientific Diagram","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Knowledge-Schema-Graph-of-D3_fig1_2884932"},{"title":"D3 gallery / D3 | Observable","url":"https://observablehq.com/@d3/gallery"},{"title":"GitHub - UNDP-Data/fe-knowledge-graph-d3-library","url":"https://github.com/UNDP-Data/fe-knowledge-graph-d3-library"},{"title":"Top 10 JavaScript Libraries for Knowledge Graph Visualization","url":"https://www.getfocal.co/post/top-10-javascript-libraries-for-knowledge-graph-visualization"},{"title":"D3.js - GeeksforGeeks","url":"https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/javascript/d3-js/"},{"title":"D3.js Visualization for Beginners | by Ebo Jackson | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@ebojacky/d3-js-visualization-for-beginners-e757b216d3d7"},{"title":"D3.js Tutorial – Data Visualization for Beginners","url":"https://www.freecodecamp.org/news/d3js-tutorial-data-visualization-for-beginners/"}]

Here are the search results for "Quartz knowledge graph visualization D3 implementation":

## Key Findings

**D3.js for Knowledge Graph Visualization:**
A typical method of using D3.js to visualize knowledge graphs (relational data) is to use force-graph. 2D and 3D visualization of knowledge graphs (relational data) and relational heat maps can be created using D3 and React for digital transformation, artificial intelligence, and machine learning tasks.

**D3.js Overview:**
D3.js is the go-to JavaScript library for interactive complex data visualizations that is flexible, handles big datasets, and has a strong community. D3 (short for Data-Driven Documents) is a JavaScript library for producing dynamic, interactive data visualizations in web browsers that makes use of Scalable Vector Graphics (SVG), HTML5, and Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) standards.

**Alternative Libraries:**
The search results also identified several other JavaScript libraries for knowledge graph visualization, including GoJS, yFiles, Cytoscape.js, and VivaGraphJS, each with different strengths for specific use cases.

**GitHub Resource:**
There is a UNDP-Data/fe-knowledge-graph-d3-library GitHub repository that provides ForceGraph implementations with configurable node and link styling for knowledge graph visualizations.

Note: The search results don't contain specific information about "Quartz" in the context of knowledge graph visualization, so this may refer to a specific tool or project that wasn't captured in these results.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about D3.js force simulation minimal code and KB size:

## Minimal Code Example

A basic D3.js force simulation can be created with: `const simulation = d3.forceSimulation(nodes) .force("charge", d3.forceManyBody()) .force("link", d3.forceLink(links)) .force("center", d3.forceCenter());`

## Getting Started

To use this module, create a simulation for an array of nodes and apply the desired forces. The simulator starts automatically; use simulation.on to listen for tick events as the simulation runs.

## Installation/Loading Options

For using d3-force in your project, you have several options:

- If you use npm, npm install d3-force.
- You can also load d3-force's UMD bundle from an npm-based CDN such as jsDelivr

## File Size Information

Unfortunately, the search results don't contain specific KB size information for the d3-force library or minimal code implementations. To get the exact file size, you would need to check the npm package page directly or the minified/gzipped bundle sizes on jsDelivr or similar CDN sources.

Web search results for query: "force-directed graph vanilla JavaScript SVG without D3"

Links: [{"title":"ForceDirected.js","url":"https://philogb.github.io/jit/static/v20/Docs/files/Visualizations/ForceDirected-js.html"},{"title":"Springy - A force directed graph layout algorithm in JavaScript.","url":"http://getspringy.com/"},{"title":"D3 Without SVG - Jim Vallandingham","url":"https://vallandingham.me/d3_without_svg.html"},{"title":"Cytoscape.js","url":"https://js.cytoscape.org/"},{"title":"D3 by Observable | The JavaScript library for bespoke data visualization","url":"https://d3js.org/"},{"title":"Directed graph editor · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/ivanlanin/9030087"},{"title":"Force-Directed Graph · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/mbostock/4062045"},{"title":"d3-force | D3 by Observable","url":"https://d3js.org/d3-force"},{"title":"Interactive & Dynamic Force-Directed Graphs with D3 | by Robin Weser | NinjaConcept | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/ninjaconcept/interactive-dynamic-force-directed-graphs-with-d3-da720c6d7811"},{"title":"d3.js Force Directed Graph: Updating Nodes and Links","url":"https://groups.google.com/g/d3-js/c/Qry7q6NQCXo"}]

Here are the search results for creating force-directed graphs in vanilla JavaScript with SVG without D3:

## Key Resources Found

**Springy.js** - Springy.js is designed to be small and simple, providing an abstraction for graph manipulation and layout calculation, with drawing and interaction details left to you, allowing you to use canvas, SVG, WebGL, or positioned HTML elements.

**JIT (JavaScript InfoVis Toolkit)** - A visualization that lays graphs using a Force-Directed layout algorithm.

**Cytoscape.js** - A graph theory (network) library for visualisation and analysis that supports many different graph theory usecases including directed graphs, undirected graphs, mixed graphs, loops, multigraphs, and compound graphs.

## Why Not Just D3?

If you specifically want to avoid D3, the Springy.js library seems like the best fit since it's designed to be small and simple, provides graph manipulation and layout calculation, and lets you handle the drawing details yourself using SVG or other rendering methods.

For a purely vanilla JavaScript approach, you could also implement a force-directed layout algorithm from scratch using basic physics principles (repulsive and attractive forces), though that would require more development effort.

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - jsongraph/json-graph-specification: A proposal for representing graph structure (nodes / edges) in JSON. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/jsongraph/json-graph-specification"},{"title":"Export Your Knowledge Graph JSON File – Nodus Labs Support Center","url":"https://support.noduslabs.com/hc/en-us/articles/12640098588700-Export-Your-Knowledge-Graph-JSON-File"},{"title":"Build a FAIR Knowledge Graph | IDS Best Practices","url":"https://maastrichtu-ids.github.io/best-practices/blog/2021/03/18/build-a-kg/"},{"title":"The Anatomy of a Content Knowledge Graph | Schema App Solutions","url":"https://www.schemaapp.com/schema-markup/the-anatomy-of-a-content-knowledge-graph/"},{"title":"Understanding Knowledge Graphs · Blue Brain Nexus","url":"https://bluebrainnexus.io/docs/getting-started/understanding-knowledge-graphs.html"},{"title":"JSON Format (ELK)","url":"https://eclipse.dev/elk/documentation/tooldevelopers/graphdatastructure/jsonformat.html"},{"title":"Google Knowledge Graph Search API | Google for Developers","url":"https://developers.google.com/knowledge-graph"},{"title":"Knowledge Graphs. This is a tutorial based on this… | by Dr. Nimrita Koul | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@nimritakoul01/knowledge-graphs-f51adb293401"},{"title":"Paul’s notes on how JSON-LD works | by Paul Frazee | Medium","url":"https://paulfrazee.medium.com/pauls-notes-on-how-json-ld-works-965732ea559d"},{"title":"How I construct hypergraphs from JSON-LD knowledge graph data with simple examples | by Philippe Höij | The Semantic Data Practitioner | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/the-semantic-data-practitioner/how-i-construct-hypergraphs-from-knowledge-graph-elements-with-json-ld-b34fec501ab3"}]

Based on the web search results, here's comprehensive information about knowledge graph JSON format specifications for nodes and edges:

## JSON Graph Specification

A proposal for representing graph structure (nodes / edges) in JSON is documented in the JSON Graph Specification. Here are the key components:

### Nodes Structure

A nodes object/Map represents nodes in a graph. Each key in the nodes object is the unique identifier for the node. The node object is the value the Map key.

Node objects can include:
- label property provides a text display for an object. Its value is defined as a JSON string.
- metadata property allows for custom data on an object. Its values is defined as a JSON object.

### Edges Structure

Edges are an array of objects, each of which represents an edge in the graph.

Edge objects contain:
- source property references the key value of the source node object. Its value is defined as a JSON string.
- target property references the key value of the target node object. Its value is defined as a JSON string.
- relation property provides the interaction between source and target nodes. Its value is defined as a JSON string.
- directed property provides the edge mode (e.g. directed or undirected). Its value is JSON true for directed and JSON false for undirected.

### Graph-Level Properties

nodes property provides the nodes in the graph. Its value is an Map/Dictionary of node objects - the Map key being the node identifier.

edges property provides the edges in the graph. Its value is an array of edge objects.

### Alternative Formats

The most widely adopted format is JSON-LD, which JSON-LD, or JSON for Linked Data, is a serialization format for expressing RDF triples. It is relatively easy for humans to read and write and also for machines to consume.

Web search results for query: "popover preview hover CSS only vs JavaScript implementation"

Links: [{"title":"Triggering a Popover with Pure CSS (No JavaScript Required) - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/ivordev/triggering-a-popover-with-pure-css-no-javascript-required-12eb"},{"title":"Using the Popover API - Web APIs | MDN","url":"https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Popover_API/Using"},{"title":"Popover and dialog | web.dev","url":"https://web.dev/learn/css/popover-and-dialog"},{"title":"Using the Popover API for HTML Tooltips – Frontend Masters Blog","url":"https://frontendmasters.com/blog/using-the-popover-api-for-html-tooltips/"},{"title":"Popover API - Web APIs | MDN","url":"https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Popover_API"},{"title":"HTML popover global attribute - HTML | MDN","url":"https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Global_attributes/popover"},{"title":"Pure CSS popover on hover","url":"https://codepen.io/chocochip/pen/zYxMgRG"},{"title":"Introducing the popover API | Blog | Chrome for Developers","url":"https://developer.chrome.com/blog/introducing-popover-api"},{"title":"Building a Popover with only CSS","url":"https://www.kallmanation.com/building-a-popover-with-only-css"},{"title":"Building a Popover with only CSS - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/kallmanation/building-a-popover-with-only-css-1085"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of popover preview hover implementations using CSS-only versus JavaScript approaches:

## CSS-Only Approaches

CSS alone can often suffice for simple interactions. There are several techniques:

**Using the :hover Pseudo-Class**: The popover could be done as a hover effect using :hover, with divs or spans instead of details elements.

**Using the :has() Selector**: The :has pseudo-class can target a label and check if it contains a checked checkbox. When the checkbox is checked, the popover's display property is set to block.

**Limitations**: This approach is mainly for demonstration. For more robust and accessible solutions, combining CSS with JavaScript or using built-in browser APIs is recommended for better control and accessibility.

## Modern Popover API (Browser Native)

The Popover API provides developers with a standard, consistent, flexible mechanism for displaying popover content on top of other page content. Popover content can be controlled either declaratively using HTML attributes, or via JavaScript.

**For Hover Effects**: Hover behavior cannot be done in HTML alone, though it could be done in CSS with animation delay, but it's likely more straightforward in JavaScript. Hint popovers are usually shown and hidden in response to non-click JavaScript events such as mouseover/mouseout and focus/blur.

## JavaScript Implementation

You can control a popover using JavaScript, which is useful when you want to show a popover in response to something besides a user's click on a button. To do this, you need to get the popover element, and then call showPopover(), hidePopover(), or togglePopover().

In summary, while simple CSS-only popovers are possible for basic cases, JavaScript is recommended for hover-triggered preview popovers to achieve proper delays, positioning, and accessibility.

Based on the search results, I found some relevant information about force-directed graph implementations with SVG:

## Key Findings

A minimal force-directed graph SVG implementation starts with an SVG element and uses D3.js. The simulation is set up with D3's forceSimulation and nodes data, and circle positions are updated to reflect node positions on each tick of the simulation through a function that sets the cx and cy attributes.

For the force physics, the charge force treats each node as a charged particle, so nodes repel each other when they get too close together, and these forces are added to the simulation through simulation.force() with named force arguments.

The most detailed resource appears to be [Tom Roth's "Force directed graph: minimal working example"](https://tomroth.dev/fdg-minimal/), which provides a step-by-step walkthrough of implementing a force-directed graph with D3.js. However, the specific line count for a minimal implementation isn't explicitly mentioned in the search results.

There's also a classic algorithmic approach based on Hooke's law and Coulomb's law, where graph nodes are imagined as charged particles repelling each other and edges become springs, which can be implemented from scratch with minimal code if you implement the physics manually rather than using a library.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "vanilla JavaScript force-directed graph algorithm implementation example"

Links: [{"title":"Springy - A force directed graph layout algorithm in JavaScript.","url":"http://getspringy.com/"},{"title":"ForceDirected.js","url":"https://philogb.github.io/jit/static/v20/Docs/files/Visualizations/ForceDirected-js.html"},{"title":"GitHub - dhotson/springy: A force directed graph layout algorithm in JavaScript","url":"https://github.com/dhotson/springy"},{"title":"GitHub - vasturiano/force-graph: Force-directed graph rendered on HTML5 canvas · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/vasturiano/force-graph"},{"title":"layout-force | Graphology","url":"https://graphology.github.io/standard-library/layout-force.html"},{"title":"Stardust Example: Force-directed Graph","url":"https://stardustjs.github.io/examples/graph/"},{"title":"GitHub - anvaka/ngraph.forcelayout: Force directed graph layout","url":"https://github.com/anvaka/ngraph.forcelayout"},{"title":"The Best Libraries and Methods to Render Large Force-Directed Graphs on the Web | by Stephen | Medium","url":"https://weber-stephen.medium.com/the-best-libraries-and-methods-to-render-large-network-graphs-on-the-web-d122ece2f4dc"},{"title":"ParaGraphL | WebGL-powered Parallel Large-scale Graph Layout Visualization Framework","url":"https://nblintao.github.io/ParaGraphL/"},{"title":"GitHub - HQarroum/directed-graph: :busstop: An implementation of a directed graph in Javascript. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/HQarroum/directed-graph"}]

Here are the search results for vanilla JavaScript force-directed graph algorithm implementation examples:

## Key Libraries and Examples

**Springy.js** - A force directed graph layout algorithm that uses a ForceDirected class where you can tune parameters like spring stiffness, node repulsion, and damping. You can create a graph, add nodes and edges with simple JavaScript API.

**ngraph.forcelayout** - A force directed graph layout algorithm that works in any dimension and uses quad tree to speed up computation of long-distance forces. Force directed algorithms are iterative and require multiple iterations before the graph looks good, with methods to get node positions after stepping through the layout.

**force-graph** - A force-directed graph rendered on HTML5 canvas that uses d3-force for the underlying physics engine and supports canvas zooming/panning and node interactions.

## Core Concepts

Force-directed graphs use physical simulation with attractive forces that pull connected nodes together and repulsive forces that push all nodes away from each other, resulting in related nodes being close together and unrelated nodes spaced apart.

For detailed implementation examples, you can visit the Springy.js GitHub repository (https://github.com/dhotson/springy) or the ngraph.forcelayout repository (https://github.com/anvaka/ngraph.forcelayout), both of which provide working code examples.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, I found information about Quartz's graph implementation:

Quartz's graph.inline.ts imports force-related functions from D3, including forceRadial, along with other D3 utilities like select, drag, and zoom. The file also imports rendering libraries from Pixi.js and Tween.js.

The search results confirm that Quartz uses D3 for its force-directed graph implementation, specifically leveraging D3's force simulation capabilities. While the search results don't provide the complete implementation details, the imports shown indicate that Quartz is using **d3-force** rather than a custom implementation for the force-directed physics simulation.

If you need more specific details about the exact force configuration or custom modifications Quartz applies to d3-force, you might want to examine the full source code directly in the [Quartz repository](https://github.com/jackyzha0/quartz/blob/v4/quartz/components/scripts/graph.inline.ts).


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Minimal Force-Directed Graph Requirements

## Dependencies
One external library is required: **D3.js v4** (or later). The example uses `https://d3js.org/d3.v4.min.js`.

## Core Code Components

Based on the tutorial, the essentials include:

1. **HTML skeleton** with an SVG element
2. **Node data** array (6 nodes in the example)
3. **Force simulation setup** with at least two forces:
   - Center force (`d3.forceCenter()`)
   - Charge force (`d3.forceManyBody()`)
4. **Circle rendering** for nodes
5. **Tick handler function** to update positions each frame

## Line Count Estimate

The minimal working implementation spans roughly **60-80 lines** of code, including:
- Basic HTML structure (~10 lines)
- CSS styling (~8 lines)
- JavaScript initialization (~5 lines)
- Data definition (~10 lines)
- Simulation creation and force setup (~10 lines)
- Node drawing (~8 lines)
- Tick update function (~8 lines)

To add links (recommended for a complete graph), add another ~15-20 lines for link data, drawing, and position updates.
